purpose.summary: This playbook is the operating system for Value Truck Account Managers. It is not a suggestion list — it is the standard. Every section defines exactly what you do, when you do it, how you do it, and what great looks like.
purpose.body:
Your job is not to "manage" accounts. Your job is to become so embedded in your customer's supply chain that replacing you would be painful, expensive, and unthinkable.

The Value Truck AM Formula: Speed + Solutions + Consistency + Relationship Depth = Irreplaceable

This playbook drives five outcomes:
1. Consistency — Every account gets the same world-class operating rhythm
2. Accountability — Clear metrics, clear expectations, no gray area
3. Revenue Growth — Proactive lane expansion, wallet share capture, referral generation
4. Relationship Depth — Move every contact from transactional to trusted partner
5. Retention — Customers stay because leaving would hurt them

items: Retain every account. Zero preventable churn.
Grow every account. More lanes, more loads, more revenue, more contacts.
Respond faster than anyone. 4 minutes or less — WITH a rate and a solution. Not "let me check."
Know your freight cold. Load count, revenue, margin, trends — daily. No surprises.
Build relationships so deep that price becomes secondary.
Coordinate flawlessly with LMs, LCs, and leadership internally.
Maintain a CRM that tells the full story of every account, every contact, every opportunity.
You are not a load coordinator. You own the relationship and growth — ops handles execution details.
You are not reactive. If you're only responding to inbound requests, you're losing.
You are not a rate machine. You sell trust, speed, and certainty — not the lowest number.
GOOD: Responds within 4 minutes consistently, CRM updated daily, hits load count and margin targets, retains assigned accounts, completes all daily/weekly cadence items on time.
GREAT: Grows accounts 15%+ QoQ, has 50%+ of contacts at 2nd base or higher, proactively identifies and pitches new lanes, generates 1+ referral per quarter, leads QBRs customers look forward to.

ELITE: Grows accounts 25%+ QoQ consistently, 75%+ of contacts at 3rd base or homerun, gets first access to RFPs and projects, customers call for market intel and advice, produces warm introductions that turn into new accounts, price is rarely the conversation.

checklist: Get the full download from the sales rep: lanes, volumes, contacts, SOPs, service requirements, pain points
Research the customer: website, genlogs, news, acquisitions, site locations, inbound/outbound patterns
Identify the customer's current core carriers and what makes them "core"
Know their annual freight volume or weekly load count — ideally broken out by site
Set up the account in CRM and TMS before the call happens
Onboarding call: AM, Sales Rep, Ops Lead, and ideally a Value Truck leader
Confirm lanes, volumes, and service expectations
Discuss communication preferences — email, phone, Teams, portal? How often? What triggers an escalation?
Stake your claim: "We are extremely good at [specific strength]. Send us the tough ones."
Set expectation: "You'll hear from me daily. If something goes wrong, you'll hear from me before you have to ask."
Ask: "What does a perfect carrier partner look like to you?"
Send follow-up email recapping the call, confirming contacts, and including the team DL
Create the internal team DL for the account
Document everything in CRM: contacts, SOPs, preferences, KPIs, communication cadence
Begin building the customer org chart — who reports to whom, who controls what freight
Send your first rate or solution on an open load — even if it's a loss leader

scripts.label: Onboarding Call Introduction
scripts.body: Hi [Name], this is [Your Name] from Value Truck. I'm your dedicated account manager and I'm going to be your main point of contact. We're extremely good at [specific strength]. That's where I want to prove ourselves first. Tell me about your biggest headaches in freight right now. What keeps you up at night?

scripts.label: Onboarding Follow-Up Email
scripts.body:
Subject: Great Connecting — Value Truck Account Summary & Next Steps

Hi [Name],

Great talking with you today. Excited to get started. Here's a quick recap:

Your Value Truck Team:
- Account Manager: [Name] — [Phone] — [Email]
- Operations Lead: [Name] — [Phone] — [Email]
- Team Email (for urgent after-hours): [DL]

What We Confirmed:
- Primary lanes: [List]
- Communication preference: [Email/Phone/Teams/Portal]
- Update frequency: [Daily/Per Load]
- Key focus: [What we're proving first]

Next Steps:
- Send us your first load(s) — we're ready to execute.
- I'll follow up [tomorrow/Monday] with rates on [lanes discussed].

Looking forward to earning your business every day.

[Your Name]

30: Complete onboarding call and full CRM setup (Day 1-2)
Move first load within 5 business days — even at breakeven or a small loss. Relevance > margin early.
Call every contact daily. Not just email. Pick up the phone.
Respond to every quote/request in under 4 minutes WITH a rate and solution.
Send proactive updates on every load — don't wait for them to ask.
Ask for the hardest freight: "What lane gives you the most headaches? Let us take a shot at it."
Document freight patterns: spot vs. contract, peak days, recurring lanes, seasonal surges.
Build initial org chart: who reports to whom, who controls what freight, who are the influencers.
Use genlogs + customer website to identify other sites, inbound/outbound patterns, and hidden freight.
SCORECARD: First load moved, all contacts at 1st base minimum, CRM fully built out, customer org chart started, response time under 4 min consistently, proactive research completed
60: Conduct first monthly business review (QBR) with the customer
Present a lane performance scorecard: on-time %, cost vs. market, communication score
Pitch 2-3 new lanes based on your research (genlogs, site map, inbound/outbound patterns)
Ask: "Who else on your team handles freight that I should connect with?"
Start transitioning spot freight to contracted freight where possible
Learn what makes each contact look good to their boss — then help them achieve it
Begin personalizing communication — memes, gifs, texts, Teams messages. Be human.
Tell on yourself: Share a specific success story.
SCORECARD: First QBR completed, 2+ contacts at 2nd base, new lane pitched or won, load count growing vs. first 30 days, any spot-to-contract conversions, new contact identified or introduced
90: Schedule or complete an in-person visit (lunch, site tour, or face-to-face meeting)
Propose taking over lanes where incumbents are failing (service issues or overpriced)
Request a warm introduction to another load planner, site, or division
Identify and propose a contractual lane commitment with volume targets
Conduct second QBR with a growth roadmap: "Here's where I think we can help you next quarter."
Ask for a referral: "Who else in your network would benefit from this kind of service?"
Begin positioning for upcoming RFPs or project freight

SCORECARD: In-person visit completed or scheduled, 1+ contacts at 3rd base, contracted lane(s) won, revenue growth of 25%+ vs. first 30 days, referral requested, warm introduction to new contact or site, positioned for upcoming RFP or project freight

daily: Check overnight load statuses — any issues? Get ahead of them before the customer wakes up.
Review today's pickups and deliveries across all accounts.
Check your numbers: load count, revenue, margin — YTD, MTD, WTD. KNOW YOUR FREIGHT.
Identify your top 3 proactive actions for the day (new lane pitch, follow-up, research, referral ask).
Call every active contact. Not email — call. The "what else" question happens on the phone, not in a subject line.
Respond to every inbound request in 4 minutes or less — WITH a rate and a solution.
Send proactive load updates before customers ask.
Escalate issues immediately. Call first, then email to document.
Coordinate with LMs and LCs on coverage, capacity, and any in-transit issues.
Update CRM: log every call, note, opportunity, and relationship movement.
Check: Did I move the needle on at least one relationship today?
Check: Did I make at least one proactive growth move (new lane pitch, referral ask, research action)?
Prep for tomorrow: anything time-sensitive first thing?
weekly: Account Health Check: Review every account's weekly load count, revenue, and margin vs. targets.
Relationship Audit: Where is every contact on the baseball diamond? What's the plan to advance each one?
Growth Pipeline: Update your opportunity pipeline — new lanes identified, pitched, won, or lost.
Weekly Summary to Leadership: One-page summary per account (load count vs. target, revenue and margin, key wins and service issues, growth opportunities in pipeline, relationship status).
Competitive Intel: Note any competitor activity you've picked up on (lost lanes, rate undercutting, service failures).
Send Customer Touchpoints: Weekly performance snapshot to key contacts (on-time %, loads moved, market insights).
Morning sync with Load Managers (LMs) and Load Coordinators (LCs).
Flag growth opportunities that need leadership support.
Report any at-risk accounts and your recovery plan.
monthly: QBR Prep & Delivery: Conduct a structured business review with every active account.
Wallet Share Analysis: What % of this customer's total freight do we have? What's the total available? Set a target.
Deep Research: Refresh genlogs, news, acquisitions, new site openings for each account.
Org Chart Update: Add new contacts, update titles, identify untapped decision-makers.
Lane Expansion Plan: Identify 2-3 new lanes to pitch per account based on research.
Referral Campaign: Ask your 3rd-base and homerun contacts for one warm introduction this month.
Personal Development: Review one loss, one win, and one growth miss. What did you learn?

signals: Fresh introduction. Calls and emails still feel formal and a little uncomfortable.
We provide options and solutions quickly from the first opportunity.
The relationship is 100% transactional — we're one of many carriers quoting loads.
Revenue is sporadic.
actions: Execute flawlessly on every load. Zero excuses. Service failures at this stage are fatal.
Respond in under 4 minutes, every time. Prove you're faster than their other options.
Get freight moving — even a loss leader. Trust is built by doing, not talking.
Find and solve a pain point they didn't ask you to solve.
Stake your claim: "We are extremely good at [specific thing]. Let us prove it."
Tell on yourself: Share a specific success story.
TIME TARGET: Every contact should reach 2nd base within 30-45 days of first interaction.

signals: We've built trust through consistent execution.
Communication is more personal — memes, gifs, texts, Teams messages. It feels natural.
You know their interests outside of work. You know what makes them look good to their boss.
Freight is still mostly transactional, but there's opportunity to convert spot to contract.
They may introduce you to other team members who need help.
Revenue is steadier.
actions: Schedule an in-person visit or lunch. Face time changes everything in freight.
Ask directly: "What lane is giving you the most trouble right now? Let me take it over."
Propose converting 2-3 spot lanes to contract with a volume commitment.
Start texting/calling from your personal cell. Make it easy for them to reach you.
Ask for a warm introduction: "Who else on your team should I be connected with?"
Deliver a QBR that isn't just data — bring insights, market intel, and a growth proposal.
Bail them out of a jam. The relationship-defining moments happen when things go wrong.
TIME TARGET: 2nd to 3rd base should take 30-60 additional days of consistent execution.

signals: You're a trusted carrier. You've bailed them out more than once and they remember.
You're texting and calling from your cell phone. You've met in person (or have firm plans to).
You have the opportunity to take over lanes where incumbents are failing — on service or price.
You're starting to become an extension of their supply chain.
You get transparency into projects, chunks of freight, and freight that never hits an RFP.
Freight is NOT just transactional. It's turning contractual with chunks of volume.
Price starts to become secondary. They value reliability and the relationship.
They give you warm introductions to other planners, even ones without huge needs.
Revenue is chunky and growing.
actions: Become their market advisor. Share rate trends, capacity shifts, and seasonal intel proactively.
Ask for a seat at the table: "When your next RFP comes up, I'd love to be in the conversation early."
Propose a strategic partnership: dedicated capacity, volume discounts, or managed lane programs.
Get introduced to leadership on their side. Move beyond load planners to directors and VPs.
Deliver so consistently that they start defending you in their internal meetings.
Start attending their industry events or inviting them to yours.

signals: This person has become a genuine friend.
You are absolutely an extension of their supply chain. They call you for market updates, eyes and ears, advice.
You get first access to projects, newly acquired lanes, site openings, and surges.
You get feedback on RFPs that only a handful of carriers receive.
You're talked about in a positive light in their team meetings. Hot introductions flow naturally.
Price is secondary. We are more than just a rate. Trust is proven and earned.
You're dealing with projects, surges, and chunks of freight.
Revenue is consistent and substantial.
actions: Never take it for granted. Homerun relationships need continuous investment.
Even a week of PTO can cool a 3rd-base relationship. Have a bulletproof handoff plan.
Continue delivering insights and market intel — don't go on autopilot.
Find ways to add value beyond freight: introductions, industry knowledge, problem-solving.
Ask for referrals consistently. Homerun contacts are your best source of new business.
Celebrate wins together. Send a note when they get promoted or their company hits a milestone.

growthLevers: LANE EXPANSION: Use genlogs, customer websites, and site maps to find lanes they haven't offered you yet. Ask directly: "Can you help me find out who handles the outbound for your [City] site?" Research inbound and outbound at every facility. Look at similar customers in the same industry.
WALLET SHARE CAPTURE: Know the total loads/week across ALL carriers. Know your share. Set a target. Identify where competitors are underperforming — that's your opportunity.

SPOT-TO-CONTRACT CONVERSION: Every recurring spot lane is a contract opportunity. After running 10+ loads on a lane, propose a rate agreement. Contract freight = predictable revenue + deeper relationship + harder for competitors to displace.
COMPETITIVE DISPLACEMENT: Never badmouth competitors. Listen for signals: late trucks, claims, poor communication, rate spikes. Propose a trial: 5-10 loads, specific KPIs, head-to-head comparison.
REFERRAL GENERATION: You earn the right to ask for referrals at 3rd base and homerun. Ask after a specific win. Track every referral in CRM. Close the loop with the person who referred you.

agenda: Performance Scorecard: on-time %, loads moved, cost vs. market rate, service incidents
Value Delivered: cost savings, emergency coverage, capacity guarantees
Market Intelligence: rate trends in their key lanes, capacity outlook, seasonal forecasts
Growth Roadmap: new lanes proposed, wallet share targets, upcoming opportunities
The Ask: specific freight you want, contacts you want to meet, RFP visibility you want
End with a "Next 90 Days" slide showing exactly what you plan to deliver

scripts.label: QBR Opening — Request to Review Data
scripts.body: I pulled together our first 30 days of data. Can I walk you through it? I have some ideas on where we can add more value.

scripts.label: QBR Lane Expansion Pitch
scripts.body: I noticed you're running freight out of [City]. We have strong capacity there. Can I take a look at those lanes?

scripts.label: QBR Elite Close — Next 90 Days Commitment
scripts.body: Here's exactly what we're committing to over the next 90 days: [specific deliverables, lane targets, service improvements]. I want you to hold us to this.

triggers: TIER 1: Late pickup or delivery (under 2 hours), tracking/update requests, minor rate disputes or accessorial questions, driver communication issues
TIER 2: Service failure on a high-value or high-visibility load, repeated service issues on the same lane or account, freight claims under $5,000, carrier no-shows requiring backup truck
TIER 3: Account at risk of churning, claims over $5,000, customer leadership has escalated internally, systemic pattern of failures (3+ issues in 30 days)
The customer should NEVER discover a problem before you tell them.
Call first. Then email to document. Never lead with email on a service failure.
Come with the problem AND the solution. Never call just to deliver bad news.
steps: TIER 1 — AM Handles (Response: Immediate): Resolve, communicate to customer within 15 minutes, log in CRM.
TIER 2 — AM + Ops Leadership (Response: Within 30 Minutes): Loop in ops leadership, provide customer with a written recovery plan within 2 hours.
TIER 3 — Executive Escalation (Response: Within 1 Hour): Value Truck leadership calls the customer directly. Written root cause + corrective action plan within 24 hours.

SERVICE RECOVERY CALL: 1) Own It — state the specific issue. 2) Explain briefly — 1-2 sentences, no excuses. 3) Solve It — specific corrective action. 4) Prevent It — systemic fix. 5) Rebuild — specific next step to earn back confidence.
After a Tier 2 or Tier 3 recovery, send a written summary within 24 hours: "What happened. What we did. What we changed. What you can expect going forward."
Log every escalation in CRM with: date, issue, root cause, resolution, time to resolve.
Review escalation trends monthly. Bring escalation data to your QBRs.

fields: Company overview: industry, size, annual freight spend (estimated if needed)
All contacts with: name, title, role, phone, email, baseball diamond stage, personal notes
Org chart: who reports to whom, who controls what freight
Freight profile: lanes, modes, volumes (spot vs. contract), peak seasons, special requirements
Communication preferences: email, phone, Teams, portal? Frequency? Escalation triggers?
Customer KPIs: what they measure us on (on-time %, communication, cost, flexibility)
SOPs: pickup/delivery requirements, accessorials, appointment scheduling, BOL handling
Growth pipeline: lanes being pursued, wallet share target, upcoming RFPs
Competitive landscape: who else hauls for them, where competitors are strong/weak
Escalation history: what went wrong, how we fixed it, what changed
rules: Log every customer call (even 2-minute check-ins) with: who, what was discussed, next action
Update any contact's relationship stage (baseball diamond) if it changed
Log any new opportunity: new lane, new contact, RFP intel, referral lead
Document any service issue and resolution
The 'Bus Test': If you got hit by a bus tomorrow, could another AM pick up your accounts on Monday and not miss a beat? If the answer is no, your CRM isn't done.
If it's not in the CRM, it didn't happen.

kpiScorecard.target: Good: <10 min | Great: <4 min | Elite: <2 min

coachingCadence.frequency: Weekly 1:1 (30 min) + Monthly team review (60 min) + Quarterly skill development
agenda: WEEKLY 1:1 — Numbers: Load count, revenue, margin vs. targets. Where are you? Where should you be?
WEEKLY 1:1 — Accounts: Walk through your top 3 accounts. What's the plan this week for growth?
WEEKLY 1:1 — Relationships: Which contacts advanced on the diamond? Which are stuck? Why?
WEEKLY 1:1 — Pipeline: What new opportunities are you pursuing? What's stalled?
WEEKLY 1:1 — Blockers: What do you need from leadership to move faster?
WEEKLY 1:1 — One Win + One Miss: What went well this week? What did you learn from a miss?
MONTHLY — Each AM presents their top growth account and their biggest challenge account.
MONTHLY — Team brainstorms solutions and shares what's working in their own accounts.
MONTHLY — Review team-level KPIs: total load count, revenue, retention, relationship distribution.
MONTHLY — Recognize top performers. Share specific examples of elite behavior.
QUARTERLY — Call listening/review: Listen to 2-3 customer calls per AM.
QUARTERLY — QBR dry runs: Practice QBR presentations before delivering to customers.
QUARTERLY — Role play: Practice service recovery calls, referral asks, competitive displacement pitches.
QUARTERLY — Market knowledge: Ensure AMs understand macro freight trends affecting their customers.
managerActions: Coach to the playbook, not to your personal style. The system works when everyone runs it.
Inspect what you expect. If you don't check CRM, it won't get updated.
Ask "How can I help?" more than "Why didn't you?"
The best AMs are built, not hired. Invest the time.

scripts.label: First Contact Call Framework
scripts.body:
1. Introduction: "Hi [Name], this is [Your Name] from Value Truck. I'm your dedicated account manager and I'm going to be your main point of contact."
2. Stake Your Claim: "We're extremely good at [specific strength]. That's where I want to prove ourselves first."
3. Learn: "Tell me about your biggest headaches in freight right now. What keeps you up at night?"
4. Ask for the Hard Stuff: "What's the toughest lane you're dealing with? Let us take a crack at it."
5. Set Expectations: "You're going to hear from me every day. I'm going to over-communicate early — I'd rather you tell me to slow down."
6. Close: "Send me everything you've got. The 'what else' mentality — that's how we operate. If you have freight, send it all."

scripts.label: Service Recovery Call Script
scripts.body:
1. Own It: "I want to be upfront with you — we had [specific issue] on load [number]."
2. Explain (briefly): "Here's what happened: [1-2 sentences, no excuses]."
3. Solve It: "Here's what we're doing right now: [specific corrective action]."
4. Prevent It: "Here's what we're changing so this doesn't happen again: [systemic fix]."
5. Rebuild: "I know we need to earn back your confidence on this. I'd like to [specific next step]."

scripts.label: Referral Ask Script
scripts.body: You've been great to work with. If you know anyone in your network who could use this level of service, I'd love a warm introduction. We just crushed it on that surge for you. Know anyone else who deals with that kind of volume? If you were going to introduce me to one person, who would it be?

scripts.label: Competitive Displacement — Trial Pitch
scripts.body: I know you've got carriers on that lane. If you ever need a backup or want to test a second option, we're here. No pressure to change anything. But if that lane ever gives you trouble, let me take a shot. No commitment. I'd love to do a 5-load trial on that lane. Let's see how we compare side by side.

scripts.label: Lane Expansion Ask
scripts.body: What else do you have? Send them all. I noticed you ship out of [City]. We have great capacity there. Can I take a look? Who handles the outbound at your [Location] facility? I'd love to connect. We've been crushing it on [lane]. Got anything else in that corridor?

scripts.label: Spot-to-Contract Conversion Pitch
scripts.body: We've been consistent on this lane for [X] loads now. Let's lock it in with a rate agreement — you get guaranteed capacity and a predictable rate, we get a committed lane. Everyone wins. We've proven ourselves on spot. Let's talk about locking in 3-5 lanes on contract so you get guaranteed capacity.

scripts.label: Daily Proactive Update Email Template
scripts.body:
Subject: Value Truck Daily Update — [Customer Name] — [Date]

Hi [Name],

Quick update on today's activity:
- [Load #]: Picked up on time at [Location]. ETA [Date/Time]. Tracking: [Link]
- [Load #]: Delivered at [Time]. POD attached.
- [Load #]: [Issue/Update]. Here's what we're doing: [Action].

Anything else I can cover for you? What else you got?

[Your Name]
[Phone] | [Email]

scripts.label: Trust-Building Power Phrases
scripts.body:
"I'd rather you hear it from me first. Here's where we stand."
"That's not going to work for you. Here's what I'd recommend instead."
"I'm going to follow up on this until it's resolved. You have my word."

scripts.label: Success Story Examples — Telling on Yourself
scripts.body:
"We covered an emergency reefer in Des Moines in 22 minutes flat last Tuesday."
"We just ran 47 loads in 3 days for a customer's surge. Zero late deliveries."
"Last quarter we saved a customer $180K by converting 12 spot lanes to contract. Let me show you how."
"If I don't tell you these stories, who will? We do this every day."
